. JavaScript has no idea we wanted to run this as a constructor — to it, this is just a plain function. And because of that, it only returns
So what's the problem with that? The moment we try to use a prototype method, we get an error.
error calling a method on undefined
, so there's no way to grab the method off it.

Most people see this as the big downside of function constructors. But if we follow a few conventions, we can dodge a lot of the trouble. For example: any function we intend to use as a constructor should have its first letter capitalized. Capitalizing the first letter makes it way easier to debug, and it's a long-standing tradition.
Any function you intend to use as a constructor should always start with a capital letter as its name.

What happens if you forget the `new` in front of a function constructor? `this` ends up pointing at `window`, the function returns `undefined`, and every prototype method becomes unreachable. This post explains why the industry convention is to capitalize the first letter of a constructor.
